patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "and why is she here ? annual exam . okay . all right . hi , sarah . how are you ?"] good . how are you ?
[Clinician asked: "i'm good . are you ready to get started ?"] yes , i am .

[Clinician asked: "okay . so sarah is a 27-year-old female here for her annual visit . so , sarah , how have you been since the last time i saw you ?"] i've been doing better . um , i've been struggling with my depression , um , a bit more just because we've been trapped really inside and remotely over the past year , so i've been struggling , um , off and on with that .
[Clinician asked: "okay . uh , and from looking at the notes , it looks like we've had you on , uh , prozac 20 milligrams a day ."] yes .
[Clinician asked: "are , are you taking that ?"] i am taking it . i think it's just a lot has been weighing on me lately .

[Clinician asked: "okay . um , and do you feel like you need an increase in your dose , or do you ... what are you thinking ? do you think that you just need to deal with some stress or you wan na try a , a different , uh , medication or ..."] i think the , the medication has helped me in the past , and maybe just increasing the dose might help me through this patch .

[Clinician asked: "okay . all right . and , and what else has been going on with you ? i know that you've had this chronic back pain that we've been dealing with . how's that , how's that going ?"] uh , i've been managing it . it's still , um , here nor there . just , just keeps , um , it really bothers me when i sit for long periods of time at , at my desk at work . so i have ... it helps when i get up and move , but it gets really stiff and it hurts when i sit down for long periods of time .
[Clinician asked: "okay , and do you get any numbing or tingling down your legs or any pain down leg versus the other ?"] a little bit of numbing , but nothing tingling or hurting down my legs .
[Clinician asked: "okay , and does the , um , do those symptoms improve when you stand up or change position ?"] yeah , it does .
[Clinician asked: "okay . all right . and any weakness in , in your legs ?"] no , no weakness , just , just the weird numbing . like , it's , like , almost like it's falling asleep on me .

[Clinician asked: "okay . and are you able to , um , do your activities of daily living ? do you exercise , go to the store , that type of thing ?"] yeah , i am . it bothers me when i'm on my feet for too long and sitting too long , just the extremes of each end .

[Clinician asked: "okay . and i know that you've had a coronary artery bypass grafting at the young age of 27 , so how's that going ?"] yeah , i had con- i had a congenital ... you know , i had a congenital artery when i was a baby , so , um , they had to do a cabg on me , um , fairly young in life , but i've been ... my heart's been doing , doing well , and arteries have been looking good .

[Clinician asked: "okay . all right , well , let's go ahead and do a quick physical exam . um , so looking at you , you do n't appear in any distress . um , your neck , there's no thyroid enlargement . uh , your heart i hear a three out of six , systolic ejection murmur , uh , that's stable . your lungs otherwise soun"] mm-hmm .

[Clinician asked: "um , and we can always give you some pain medication if you , if you get some pain periodically with activity . how do you feel about that ? do you need some pain medication ?"] no , i think physical therapy is the right way to , way to start out on this .

[Clinician asked: "okay . hey , dragon , order physical therapy referral . and then in terms of your depression , we talked about increasing your prozac , so we'll increase it from 20 milligrams to 40 milligrams . it's just one tablet once a day ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "um , and i'll send those to your pharmacy . does that sound okay ?"] that sounds great .

[Clinician asked: "hey , dragon , order prozac , 40 milligrams , once a day . and then in terms of your ... the heart bypass that you've had ... let's go ahead and just order another echocardiogram for you , and i wan na continue you on the aspirin for now , okay ?"] okay .

[Clinician asked: "hey , dragon , order an echocardiogram . hey , dragon , order aspirin 81 milligrams daily . okay , so the nurse will come in . she'll help you schedule those things , and we'll go from there , okay ?"] okay .
[Clinician asked: "all right , take care ."] thank you .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Annual visit.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

The patient is a 27-year-old female who presents for her annual visit. The patient reports that she has been doing better since her last visit. She reports that she has been struggling with her depression off and on for the past year. The patient notes that it might be due been trapped inside and remotely over the past year. She reports that she is taking Prozac 20 mg, but she believes that it has been weighing on her lately. She notes that an increase in her Prozac dose might be beneficial for her at this time.

The patient reports that she has had chronic back pain that she has been managing. She reports that she experiences stiffness and pain when she sits or stands for long periods of time at her desk at work. She reports that it helps when she gets up and moves. She reports that she has a little bit of numbness down her legs, but no tingling or pain down her legs. She reports that the symptoms improve when she stands up or changes positions. She denies any weakness in her legs.

She reports that she has had a coronary artery bypass grafting. She reports that she had a congenital artery when she was a baby and they had to do a CABG on her fairly young age. She reports that her heart has been doing well and her arteries have been looking good.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Musculoskeletal: Endorses back pain.
• Neurological: Endorses numbness in legs.
• Psychiatric: Endorses depression.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Constitutional: in no apparent distress.
• Neck: Supple without thyromegaly.
• Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: 3/6 systolic ejection murmur, stable.
• Musculoskeletal: Pain to palpation of the lumbar spine. Decreased flexion of back. Lower extremity strength is good.

RESULTS

Echocardiogram appears unchanged in comparison to last year.

X-rays of the lumbar spine stable in comparison to last year.

ASSESSMENT

The patient is a 27-year-old female who presents today for an annual followup of chronic conditions.

Chronic back pain.
• Medical Reasoning: She is experiencing worsened pain with sitting for extended periods of time.
• Medical Treatment: Physical therapy referral ordered. Patient would like to defer pain medication at this time.

Depression
• Medical Reasoning: The patient was previously doing well on Prozac 20 mg once daily but feels as though she needs a higher dose at this time.
• Medical Treatment: Increase Prozac to 40 mg once daily. Prescription submitted.

History of coronary artery bypass graft.
• Medical Reasoning: She is doing well at this time. We will continue to monitor this.
• Medical Treatment: Echocardiogram ordered. Continue aspirin 81 mg daily.

naive.response_text: Sarah, I understand how challenging the past year has been, especially with the restrictions and remote work. It's completely valid that your depression has been more pronounced during this time. I'm glad you're open to increasing your Prozac dosage; we'll monitor how that helps. For your back pain, physical therapy sounds like a great approach to help manage it without medication, which I think will be beneficial for you. Regarding your heart, it's reassuring to know everything is stable, and the echocardiogram will help us keep track of your health. Let's work together on these steps, and please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any concerns. You're doing the right things to take care of yourself, and I'm here to support you.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing all of that with me, Sarah. It makes sense that you're feeling overwhelmed with everything that's been going on over the past year. Struggling with depression during such a challenging time is completely understandable, and I want to make sure we work together to help you feel better.

For your depression, we’re going to increase your Prozac dose to 40 mg, which is a common step when symptoms feel like they’re getting worse, especially when other stressors are involved [PMID ?]. We’ll keep a close eye on how you’re feeling over the next few weeks. If things don’t improve or if you start feeling more down, we can always explore other options together.

Regarding your back pain, the fact that it improves when you stand up or move around suggests it might be related to posture or muscle tension, but the numbness you’re experiencing is something we should keep an eye on. I’ve ordered a physical therapy referral to help you manage the discomfort and improve your mobility. We’ll also schedule an echocardiogram to check in on your heart health, just to make sure everything’s continuing to look good [PMID ?].

Before you leave, could you tell me in your own words what we’ve agreed to do? That way I can make sure I’ve explained everything clearly. We’ll plan to check in with each other in about 4-6 weeks to see how things are going.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in a few weeks. In the meantime, if your back pain gets worse or you experience any new symptoms, please call us.

full.response_text:
I understand you're experiencing some concerning symptoms, particularly the numbness in your legs, which sounds unusual and worrying.

Chronic back pain can often be managed with group exercise programs, which have been shown to improve health outcomes. These programs typically last 45-60 minutes per session, 2-3 times a week for 12 weeks.

The numbness you're experiencing could be related to neurological issues. It's important to address this as it might indicate a serious underlying condition that needs further investigation.

Given the numbness in your legs, we need to prioritize further evaluation. We'll schedule additional tests to rule out any serious neurological issues.

We'll also continue to monitor your chronic back pain and explore appropriate management options, such as group exercise programs that have been shown to improve health outcomes.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to follow up in two weeks to review the test results and adjust our plan accordingly. If you experience any severe symptoms like loss of strength or worsening numbness, please call us immediately.
